questions.questionText: Staphylococcus aureus is differentiated from other staphylococci by:
questions.optionB: Coagulase production (coagulase-positive), mannitol fermentation (yellow on MSA), and golden-yellow pigment
questions.explanation: S. aureus: Gram-positive coccus in clusters. Key features: coagulase POSITIVE (most important — clots plasma, differentiates from CoNS), catalase positive, mannitol salt agar (MSA): yellow colonies (mannitol fermenter), golden pigment, protein A (binds Fc of IgG → antiphagocytic). Causes: skin infections (boils, abscesses, impetigo), endocarditis, osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, food poisoning (preformed enterotoxin), TSS (TSST-1), scalded skin syndrome (exfoliative toxin). Reference: Ananthanarayan — S. aureus.

questions.questionText: Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Strep) causes post-infection sequelae including:
questions.optionB: Rheumatic fever (by molecular mimicry — M protein) and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
questions.explanation: GAS (Strep pyogenes): Group A, β-hemolytic, bacitracin sensitive, PYR positive. Post-infectious complications (non-suppurative, immune-mediated): (1) Rheumatic fever: molecular mimicry between M protein and cardiac tissue → cross-reactive antibodies → pancarditis, polyarthritis, chorea, subcutaneous nodules, erythema marginatum (Jones criteria). ONLY after pharyngitis (NOT skin). (2) PSGN: after pharyngitis OR skin infection. ASO titer elevated. Treatment: penicillin. Reference: Ananthanarayan — S. pyogenes.

questions.optionB: Optochin sensitivity, bile solubility, lancet-shaped diplococci, and Quellung reaction
questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae: Gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci, α-hemolytic (green hemolysis on blood agar). Identified by: optochin sensitive (vs. viridans streptococci — resistant), bile soluble (vs. viridans — insoluble), Quellung reaction (capsule swelling). Polysaccharide capsule: most important virulence factor. Causes: community-acquired pneumonia (#1 cause), meningitis (adults), otitis media, sinusitis. Vaccines: PCV13 (conjugate, children), PPSV23 (polysaccharide, adults). Reference: Ananthanarayan — S. pneumoniae.

questions.questionText: Enterococcus species are significant because they:
questions.optionB: Are inherently resistant to many antibiotics (cephalosporins, aminoglycosides) and can acquire vancomycin resistance (VRE)
questions.explanation: Enterococci (E. faecalis, E. faecium): Group D streptococci (now separate genus). Intrinsic resistance: cephalosporins (all), low-level aminoglycoside, TMP-SMX (clinically). Acquired resistance: VRE (vancomycin-resistant enterococci — esp. E. faecium, D-Ala-D-Lac substitution). Causes: UTI (nosocomial), endocarditis (subacute), biliary infections, bacteremia. Treatment: ampicillin/penicillin + gentamicin (synergy) for endocarditis. VRE: linezolid, daptomycin. Grow in bile, 6.5% NaCl, PYR positive. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Enterococcus.

questions.questionText: MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus) resistance is mediated by:
questions.optionB: mecA gene encoding altered PBP2a (penicillin-binding protein 2a) with low affinity for all β-lactams
questions.explanation: MRSA: mecA gene (on SCCmec cassette) encodes PBP2a — altered penicillin-binding protein with LOW affinity for ALL β-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems) → resistant to entire class. NOT due to β-lactamase alone. Treatment: vancomycin (IV, for serious infections), linezolid, daptomycin, TMP-SMX, doxycycline (for mild skin). CA-MRSA: Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL) → necrotizing skin infections, pneumonia. VRSA: rare, vanA gene (from VRE). Reference: Ananthanarayan — MRSA.
